Proof Build DiscoveryCase Study

What Cal proves

Clarity before dispatch.

Built around the L.E. Isley & Sons Plumbing workflow, Cal demonstrates a reusable AtlasFlow pattern for field service: a messy request becomes AI triage, a structured job record, Operational Viewer visibility, human review, and a clear recommended next action.

Product thesis

Service requests arrive messy and inconsistent, forcing dispatchers to interpret urgency, risk, and next steps manually. Cal can turn that unstructured input into clear, prioritized, dispatch-ready work while directing owner attention to the requests that matter most.

Validation question

Do Cal’s core artifacts help L.E. Isley & Sons Plumbing turn messy service requests into faster, more consistent decisions by surfacing urgent or high-risk work sooner and clarifying the right next action—without removing human control?

Proof artifact

Connected to real Cal build work.

The proof artifacts show Cal’s Airtable system of record, AI-enriched dispatch fields, focused operational views, technician assignment, and the owner Morning Brief.

01

Morning Brief

Shows the owner Morning Brief record with workload summary, urgent jobs, needs review, recommended focus, and generation source.

02

AI-Enriched Requests

Shows raw requests enriched with job type, urgency, priority, RYG confidence, AI summary, and recommended next action.

03

Needs Review

Shows the focused queue for Yellow-confidence or ambiguous requests that still require office judgment.

04

Ready for Dispatch

Shows requests Cal has structured and cleared for the next dispatch step.

05

Emergency / High Priority

Shows the urgent queue where emergency and high-priority work is surfaced first.

06

RYG Confidence

Shows Red, Yellow, and Green groupings for quick review of confidence and operational risk.

07

Assigned Technician

Shows work grouped by assigned technician, including requests that still need ownership.

08

End-to-End Dispatch Proof

Shows the final dispatch proof after Cal writes structured AI fields back into the Airtable record.

Build architecture

Lean stack. Clear operating signal.

The Cal MVP keeps the stack intentionally lean and aligned to the working artifacts: Airtable stores service requests and Morning Brief records, Airtable Automations run the workflow, OpenAI performs triage and brief generation, and Airtable views plus the Cal Operational Viewer present the results for human review.

01

Form

Captures customer, phone, address, and a raw service request from a call, text, email, form, or handwritten note.

→
02

Table

Stores the raw request, structured triage outputs, workflow status, and Morning Brief-ready fields.

→
03

AI

Generates category, urgency, issue summary, customer risk, recommended action, dispatch notes, RYG confidence, and owner-attention status.

→
04

Automation

Maps and writes the structured AI output back into the original service request record.

→
05

Views

Organizes work into Emergency / High Priority, Needs Review, Ready for Dispatch, Owner Attention, and Morning Brief views.

Customer discovery

Validated against a real plumbing operator.

Doug Isley, an owner-operator and technology-forward advisor, helped ground Cal in the reality of a plumbing business already using a mature field-service platform.

Current environment

ServiceTitan + multiple add-ons

Isley Plumbing has used ServiceTitan since 2018 and spends approximately $60,000 annually. Doug’s view was that it covers most core operating needs, with customer communication already about 60% automated.

Strongest workflow reaction

Technician Note Cleaner

Doug responded most strongly to turning rough field notes into a clean office summary with parts needed, unresolved issues, and the next action. The likely daily user would be the office manager.

Design implication

Keep the MVP simple and compelling

The discovery did not reveal a broad platform gap to replace ServiceTitan. It reinforced Cal’s role as a focused proof of reusable AI workflow design—not an attempt to become an all-in-one trades platform.

What the discovery changed

Cal remains a service-request triage MVP because it clearly demonstrates messy input becoming structured action. The next discovery-led extension should test technician-note cleanup and office handoff rather than expanding into scheduling, routing, inventory, or payments.

Business value

Speed and clarity for small service teams.

For Doug’s team, Cal reduces the mental triage load on the office and gives ownership a cleaner read on what deserves attention. It is not trying to replace ServiceTitan or become a full dispatch platform; it proves the workflow that turns operational noise into clear decisions.

Morning Brief sample

Today’s owner focus

Start with active water damage and emergency drain risk. Move emergency and high-priority jobs before routine work, then review Red and Yellow confidence items before assigning technicians.

2High-risk jobs
3Needs review
5Ready to dispatch
